claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, intravitreal aflibercept given monthly or every 2 months after three initial monthly doses achieved vision outcomes similar to monthly ranibizumab.
support_summary: Direct support comes from analyses of the phase 3 VIEW trials showing that aflibercept 2 mg every 8 weeks after 3 monthly doses produced visual-function outcomes similar to monthly ranibizumab over 52 weeks, with similar NEI VFQ-25 changes across subscales (PMID: 25439429). A subgroup analysis of VIEW in Asian patients also found no significant differences in best-corrected visual acuity gains between aflibercept 2q4 or 2q8 and monthly ranibizumab through Week 96, although aflibercept showed greater anatomic drying in some measures (PMID: 29280937). Additional, less direct support includes a review summarizing phase III trials as showing comparable vision gains for aflibercept every 4 or 8 weeks versus monthly ranibizumab (PMID: 22973088), and smaller comparative studies reporting similar 12-month visual acuity outcomes between aflibercept and ranibizumab but using less exact regimens or limited sample sizes (PMID: 26166797, PMID: 27230919). One included paper is a cost-effectiveness analysis that assumes, rather than independently demonstrates, similar efficacy based on VIEW data, so it is only indirect support for the claim (PMID: 29952707).
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The strongest evidence comes from large randomized VIEW trial analyses directly showing similar visual outcomes for aflibercept monthly or every 8 weeks after three loading doses versus monthly ranibizumab, while the remaining studies are broadly consistent but more indirect or lower quality.

support_highlights.snippet: This analysis included patients who received intravitreal aflibercept 2.0 mg every 8 weeks (2q8; n=607) or ranibizumab 0.5 mg every 4 weeks (0.5q4; n=595).

Patients were randomized 1:1:1:1 to receive intravitreal aflibercept 2q8 (after 3 initial monthly doses), intravitreal aflibercept 2q4, intravitreal aflibercept 0.5q4, or ranibizumab 0.5q4 in the study eye.

Mean change from baseline to 52 weeks was similar for ranibizumab 0.5q4 and intravitreal aflibercept 2q8 across all 12 subscales, with the greatest improvements noted for mental health and general vision (9.0-11.6 points, both treatments, both studies).

Mean change from baseline to 52 weeks in NEI VFQ-25 composite score (pooled data) stratified by clinical response showed meaningful improvement only in patients who gained 5 Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy letters or more (7.3 and 7.8 points for intravitreal aflibercept 2q8 and ranibizumab 0.5q4, respectively).

Visual function outcomes were similar across all NEI VFQ-25 subscales over 52 weeks for intravitreal aflibercept 2q8 and ranibizumab 0.5q4, with clinically meaningful improvement recorded in 6 of 12 subscales.

We evaluated data from VIEW 1 and VIEW 2, comparing functional and morphologic outcomes at Week 96 between intravitreal aflibercept 2 mg monthly (2q4) or 2 mg bimonthly after 3 initial monthly doses (2q8) versus ranibizumab 0.5 mg monthly among Asian patients (n = 269) and between Asian and white patients (n = 2044).

In Asian patients, there were no significant differences between intravitreal aflibercept 2q4 and 2q8 compared with ranibizumab in mean gain in best-corrected visual acuity (10.23 and 8.35 vs. 8.51 letters).

In Asian patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, functional and morphologic outcomes were largely similar between intravitreal aflibercept and ranibizumab groups, and to results seen in white patients.

Given the similar efficacy and safety profiles between IAI and ranibizumab, their associated costs and comparative cost-effectiveness are key factors in determining which one represents a more rational investment of scarce health care resources to help address the increasing cost of prescription drugs in the United States, a source of concern for patients, prescribers, payers, and policymakers.
Over a lifetime, IAI 2q8 provided equal health benefits with Rq4 (5.44 QALYs) at a lower total cost ($33,745 vs. $48,031) as a result of fewer injections.
Phase III trials showed that monthly or every other month injections of aflibercept prevent vision loss (fewer than 15 letters) in 95% of patients.
Additionally, aflibercept injections every 4 or 8 weeks produce average vision gains of 6.9 letters to 10.9 letters, comparable with those achieved with monthly ranibizumab.
Outcomes were similar in the 2 groups over a 12-month study duration, with no statistical difference.

This comparative pilot study suggests that neovascular age-related macular degeneration requiring monthly retreatment with ranibizumab may respond in similar ways to both ranibizumab and aflibercept treatment.
Patients initially received three monthly injections, and were re-treated when neovascularization recurred.
There was no difference in BCVA between the two groups at either diagnosis (P = 0.560) or 12 months (P = 0.702).
The 12-month treatment outcome of intravitreal ranibizumab was similar to that of intravitreal aflibercept, with a comparable injection frequency.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, intravitreal aflibercept produced similar improvements in visual acuity and anatomic retinal outcomes as monthly ranibizumab.
support_summary: Several comparative studies reported broadly similar visual outcomes between aflibercept and ranibizumab in neovascular AMD. A pilot randomized comparison in patients needing monthly ranibizumab found no statistical difference in outcomes over 12 months after switching to aflibercept versus continuing ranibizumab (PMID: 26166797), and a retrospective 12-month study in treatment-naive eyes likewise found no difference in BCVA at baseline or 12 months (PMID: 27230919). In eyes with retinal pigment epithelial detachment, no significant between-group difference was seen in visual acuity change or RPED dimensions, with ~30% complete RPED resolution in each group (PMID: 28820848). VIEW subgroup analyses in Asian patients showed no significant difference in BCVA gain between aflibercept and monthly ranibizumab, although some morphologic measures favored aflibercept, creating a minor within-study discrepancy with a strict “similar anatomic outcomes” interpretation (PMID: 29280937); similarly, a real-world post-bevacizumab switch study found comparable BCVA and CMT outcomes between ranibizumab and aflibercept despite fewer injections with aflibercept (PMID: 39431040).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is dominated by switch studies in ranibizumab-insufficient responders, a population not fully matching the original claim, but several abstracts suggest outcomes are not always similar. Some studies reported aflibercept produced notable anatomic improvement without significant visual acuity gain after prior ranibizumab failure (PMID: 33567025, PMID: 24895562, PMID: 25461263, PMID: 27652915, PMID: 28393034), while others suggested aflibercept had stronger effects on certain anatomic measures such as PED or choroidal thickness than ranibizumab (PMID: 25395483, PMID: 27115852), implying non-equivalence rather than similarity. A more direct comparative study in occult CNV found greater BCVA improvement with ranibizumab at 3 months, even though aflibercept reduced some fluid-related measures faster (PMID: 26604674). Another case series described worsening subretinal fluid after switching well-controlled monthly ranibizumab eyes to aflibercept, with improvement after switching back to ranibizumab (PMID: 27354759). Some contradiction-group articles are only indirectly relevant because they lack head-to-head comparison and mainly show aflibercept benefit in refractory disease rather than true contradiction of equivalence (for example PMID: 23549101, PMID: 24362854).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported, because multiple head-to-head or comparative studies directly report similar visual acuity outcomes and broadly comparable functional/anatomic results between aflibercept and ranibizumab. However, the evidence is not uniform: several refractory-disease switch studies and at least one comparative study indicate subgroup-specific differences, especially in anatomic response and occasional superiority of one agent over the other.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, intravitreal aflibercept had a safety profile similar to monthly ranibizumab.
support_summary: Direct supportive evidence comes mainly from a VIEW trials subgroup analysis in Asian patients, which reported that serious treatment-emergent ocular adverse events occurred in fewer than 8% of treated eyes and were evenly distributed across aflibercept and ranibizumab subgroups, consistent with similar safety profiles (PMID: 29280937). A review of aflibercept for neovascular AMD states that randomized trials showed an excellent safety profile for anti-VEGF therapy and that aflibercept every 8 weeks after loading was clinically equivalent and noninferior to monthly ranibizumab, but the abstract does not provide explicit head-to-head safety event data (PMID: 23413903). Other cited studies mostly address efficacy or treatment response rather than safety: a small randomized pilot found similar outcomes after switching to aflibercept versus continuing ranibizumab but did not report safety details (PMID: 26166797); a noncomparative conversion study reported no significant adverse event with aflibercept alone, without direct comparison to ranibizumab (PMID: 26398691); and a retrospective comparative study found similar visual outcomes and injection frequency but no explicit safety comparison (PMID: 27230919). Overall, the supporting set contains one reasonably direct safety comparison and several only indirectly relevant studies, creating some evidence-quality discrepancy within the support group.
contradict_summary: The main contradicting study is a small retrospective case series describing 17 eyes with neovascular AMD that were previously well controlled on monthly ranibizumab but developed increased subretinal fluid after switching to aflibercept; fluid resolved after switching back to ranibizumab (PMID: 27354759). However, this abstract primarily reports worsening anatomic response in a selected subset rather than direct comparative safety adverse-event rates, and it also states that no eye had persistent decline in visual acuity. Thus, it suggests that aflibercept may perform worse in some patients but is only an indirect contradiction of the claim about overall safety profile similarity.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest abstract-level evidence is the VIEW subgroup analysis showing evenly distributed serious ocular adverse events between aflibercept and ranibizumab (PMID: 29280937), whereas the contradictory evidence is limited to a small selected case series focused more on anatomic worsening after switching than on overall safety.

support_highlights.snippet: Serious treatment-emergent ocular adverse events occurred in <8% of treated eyes, evenly distributed across subgroups.
Randomized trials have demonstrated the excellent safety profile and significant benefit of anti-VEGF therapy in maintaining vision.

Having received US FDA approval for neovascular AMD in November 2011, aflibercept given every 8 weeks after a loading dose was 'clinically equivalent' and statistically noninferior to the current FDA-approved therapy ranibizumab (Lucentis(®); Genentech, CA, USA), given every 4 weeks.
No significant adverse event was observed during the follow-up.

contradict_highlights.snippet: Herein, we identify a unique subset of patients whose eyes with neovascular AMD were previously well controlled with ranibizumab injections were then worsened after being switched to aflibercept.
All eyes developed increased subretinal fluid when switched from ranibizumab to aflibercept.
Fourteen patients were switched back to ranibizumab after a single injection of aflibercept and had subsequent rapid resolution of subretinal fluid.

Three patients continued with monthly aflibercept injections for two subsequent months and demonstrated the persistence of the increased subretinal fluid until they were switched back to treatment with ranibizumab at which time the fluid resolved.

Switching from intravitreal ranibizumab to aflibercept in eyes with well-controlled neovascular AMD may result in worsening in a subset of patients and resolves when therapy is switched back to ranibizumab.
